content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

April 26, 1977

Walter I. Colby
County Counsel
County of Yuba
Courthouse
Marysville, California 95901

Dear Mr. Colby:

This is in response to your letter of February 24, 1977
in which you seek advice relative to the responsibilities of
members of the Yuba County Planning Commission. Your request
is treated pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b).

The substance of your letter is repeated herein:

"The Planning Commission is now conducting
public hearings, pursuant to Government Code
Section 65854, on proposals to impose acreage
limitations on land divisions of Class 1, 2, 3
and 4 soil lands. The substance of the proposal being considered is to conserve agricultural land by imposing zoning restrictions upon
the owners' power to subdivide. It can well be
argued that such restrictions could adversely
substantially affect the value of the subject
land. There are, of course, contrary arguments.

Members of the Commission own land which could
be affected by the proposals.

The threshhold issue seems easily resolved: it
seems clear that the lands owned by some of the
Commissioners would be affected differently from
lands or other economic interests of members of
the public, because of the size of the holdings of
members of the Commission.

It should be further noted that the Planning
Commission is not the decision-making body in this
matter, but is the advisory agency. (See Section

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE ADMINISTRATION 916 322 4000 · [illegible] [illegible] · ENFORCEMENT CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322 9441

Walter I. Colby
April 26, 1977
page two

65857). The question, then, is whether or not
members of the Commission who own land which could
be affected by the decision of the Board of Super-
visors may participate in the deliberations of
the advisory agency, and vote upon the recommendations
of the advisory agency to the decision maker."

Your question turns on the interpretation of various pro-
visions within Government Code Section 87100. That section
provides: "[N]o public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in any way
attempt to use his or her official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know
he has a financial interest." (emphasis added)

I. The first issue concerns the construction of the concept
"public official". The term as defined in Government Code
Section 82048 follows:

"'Public official' means every member, officer,
employee or consultant of a state or local govern-
ment agency."

The Fair Political Practice Commission at 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18700(a)(1) has defined "member" as follows:

"'Member' shall include but not be limited to sal-
aried or unsalaried members of boards or commissions
with decision-making authority. A board or commission
possesses decision-making authority whenever:

(a) it may make a final governmental decision;

(b) it may compel a governmental decision; or it
may prevent a governmental decision either by reason
of an exclusive power to initiate the decision or by
reason of a veto which may not be overridden;..."

Pursuant to the State Planning and Zoning Law, Government
Code Section 65000 et seq., planning commissions possess signi-
ficant governmental powers which render them "decision-making"
within the meaning of the Commission's regulation, above. In
addition, planning commissions have been clothed by local supple-
menting legislation with powers beyond even those specified
in the Government Code. [See, Richtor et al. v. Board of Super-
visors of Sacramento County 259 C.A. 2d 99; 66 Cal. Rptr. 52, 1968]

Walter I. Colby
April 26, 1977
page three

In addition, the Moscow Governmental Conflict of Interest
and Disclosure Act, Government Code Section 3600 et seq., pro-
vided specifically for the inclusion of planning commissions
within the coverage of that Act. (See Government Code Sections
3610(h), 3625 and 3700). Pursuant to legislative amendment,
the Moscow Act was rendered inoperable except for provisions
relative to the disclosure responsibilities of planning commis-
sioners. Government Code Section 3801 provides:

"[m]embers of planning commissions and planning
officers of cities and counties required to file
financial disclosure statements pursuant to Sections
3700-3702, inclusive, shall continue to file statements
required by Sections 3700-3702, inclusive, until such
time as a Conflict of Interest Code prepared pursuant
to Section 87300 is effective for their agency."

This provision of the Moscow Act manifests a legislative
intent--and concurrence by the Fair Political Practices Commission 1/--that planning commissions are public agencies and their
members public officials within the meaning of the Political
Reform Act. This conclusion is compelled by the fact that only
public officials may be subject to the requirements of a conflict
of interest code adopted pursuant to Government Code Section 87300
et seq. Based upon these considerations, there appears to be
no serious argument to the inclusion of planning commissioners
within the concept "public officials" within Government Code
Sections 82048 and 87100.

II. Having concluded that members of planning commissions are
public officials, the next issue is whether planning commissions
"participate in making" governmental decisions when making recommendations pursuant to statute to the local legislative body.
The answer is yes. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(c) provides:

"(c) A public official or designated employee
'participates in the making of a governmental decision'
when, acting within the authority of his or her position,
he or she:

(1) Negotiates, without significant substan-
tial review, with a governmental entity or private
person regarding the decision; or

(2) Advises or makes recommendations to
the decision-maker, either directly or without
significant intervening substantive review, by:

1/ The FPPC supported AB 494 which amended the Moscow Act pursuant
to the provisions of Government Code §53800 et seq.

Walter I. Cole
April 26, 1977
Page four

(A) Conducting research or making
any investigation which requires the
exercise of judgment on the part of the
official or designated employee and the
purpose of which is to influence the
decision; or

(B) Preparing or presenting any
report, analysis or opinion, orally
or in writing, which requires the exercise
of judgment on the part of the official
or designated employee and the purpose
of which is to influence the decision."

Pursuant to Government Code Sections 65854-7, the Planning
Commission of Yuba County will be functioning and recommending
with respect to the decisions regarding acreage limitations
on land divisions of Class 1, 2, 3 and 4 soil lands in precisely
the manner envisioned to be within the Commission's participation regulation above.2/

2/ See, for example, Government Code Section 65855 which provides:

"After the hearing, the planning commission shall render its
decision in the form of a written recommendation to the
legislative body. Such recommendation shall include the
reasons for the recommendation, the relationship of the
proposed ordinance or amendment to applicable general and
specific plans, and shall be transmitted to the legislative
body in such form and manner as may be specified by the legis-
lative body;" and

Section 65857 which provides:

"The legislative body may approve, modify or disapprove the
recommendation of the planning commission; provided that
any modification of the proposed ordinance or amendment by
the legislative body not previously considered by the planning
commission during its hearing, shall first be referred to
the planning commission for report and recommendation....
Failure of the planning commission to report within forty
days after the reference, or such longer period as may be
designated by the legislative body, shall be deemed to be
approval of the proposed modifications."

Walter I. Colby
April 26, 1977
Page five

Thus, it must be concluded that planning commissioners are
public officials and that they participate in the making of
decisions in the instance related in your letter of February 24.

As you have indicated in your letter, the answer to these
two questions is not conclusive with respect to the necessity
for landowner, commission members to disqualify themselves from
participating in this bonding series of decisions. What must
be further determined is whether the requisite disqualifying
financial interest exists on the part of any of the commissioners.

Government Code Section 87103 provides, inter alia:

"Financial Interest. An official has a financial
interest in a decision within the meaning of Section
87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the deci-
sion will have a material financial effect, distinguish-
able from its effect on the public generally, on:

...(b) Any real property in which the public official
has a direct or indirect interest worth more than one
thousand dollars ($1,000)..."

Under the foregoing provisions, several elements must be
present before a public official is required to disqualify him-
self from participation in a governmental decision. First, it
must be reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision
will have a financial effect. Second, the anticipated financial
effect must be on a financial interest of the official as defined
in this case in Government Code Section 87103(b), above. Third,
the anticipated financial effect must be material. Fourth, the
governmental decision's anticipated financial effect on the
official's financial interest must be distinguishable from its
effect on the public generally.3/ We have no basis upon which
to conclude that all of the elements for compelling disquali-
fication pursuant to 87100 and 87103 are present. The crucial
decisions in this case, of course, involve the materiality of
the decision and the potential effect of the decision on the
public generally. We assume from your letter that because of the
size of the holdings of the member of the commission, the
"materiality" and the "effect on the public generally" elements
are satisfied.

_________________________________________________________________

3/ See Opinion requested by Tom Thorn r, on behalf of the Board
of Directors, Marin Municipal Water District, 1 FPPC Ops.
198(No. 75-089, Dec. 4, 1975).

Walter I. Colby
April 26, 1977
page six


If you have further questions with respect to this matter,
please do not hesitate to call me at 322-6444 in Sacramento.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief
Conflicts of Interest Division

DLS:mfa

sections.question: The question, then, is whether or not members of the Commission who own land which could be affected by the decision of the Board of Supervisors may participate in the deliberations of the advisory agency, and vote upon the recommendations of the advisory agency to the decision maker.
sections.conclusion: We have no basis upon which to conclude that all of the elements for compelling disqualification pursuant to 87100 and 87103 are present. The crucial decisions in this case, of course, involve the materiality of the decision and the potential effect of the decision on the public generally. We assume from your letter that because of the size of the holdings of the members of the commission, the 'materiality' and the 'effect on the public generally' elements are satisfied.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether Planning Commission members who own land that could be affected by proposed acreage limitation zoning restrictions must disqualify themselves from participating in the Commission's deliberations and voting on recommendations to the Board of Supervisors.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Planning Commission members are public officials who participate in governmental decision-making through their recommendations, and disqualification under Government Code §87100 requires that a decision have a material financial effect distinguishable from its effect on the public generally; materiality and distinguishability must be analyzed based on the specific circumstances of each commissioner's holdings.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains explicit QUESTION section quoted within the body. CONCLUSION section identified within final substantive paragraphs. Well-structured letter with clear legal analysis organized by numbered issues. No section headers but content clearly delineates Q&A structure.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The question, then, is whether or not members of the Commission who own land which could be affected by the decision of the Board of Supervisors may participate in the deliberations of the advisory agency, and vote upon the recommendations of the advisory agency to the decision maker.

CONCLUSION: We have no basis upon which to conclude that all of the elements for compelling disqualification pursuant to 87100 and 87103 are present. The crucial decisions in this case, of course, involve the materiality of the decision and the potential effect of the decision on the public generally. We assume from your letter that because of the size of the holdings of the members of the commission, the 'materiality' and the 'effect on the public generally' elements are satisfied.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that Yuba County Planning Commission members are public officials subject to conflict of interest rules, and they participate in governmental decision-making when making recommendations on land use proposals. Disqualification depends on whether the proposed zoning restrictions would have a material financial effect on individual commissioners' land holdings that is distinguishable from effects on the public generally.
